1: bĕšûb yhwh ʾet-šîbat ṣiyyôn hāyînû kĕḥōlĕmîm — "when the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion, we were like people in a dream" — šûb ʾet-šĕbût (the idiom "restore the fortunes/captivity") — the phrase appears ~27x in the OT; it likely means both "restore the fortunes" (general reversal of loss) and "return from captivity" (exile and return); both senses apply to the post-exilic community. hāyînû kĕḥōlĕmîm (we were like dreamers) — so sudden, so complete was the reversal that it seemed unreal. The simile does not mean they were asleep but that the restoration felt like the impossible come true.
2: ʾāz yimmālēʾ śĕḥôq pînû ûlĕšônênû rinnāh ʾāz yōʾmĕrû baggôyim higgîl yhwh laʿăśôt ʿim-ʾēlleh — "then our mouths were filled with laughter and our tongues with shouts of joy; then the nations were saying, 'The LORD has done great things for them!'" — śĕḥôq (laughter, joy, the sound of laughter) — not mirth at another's expense but the laughter of relief and wonder. rinnāh (shouts of joy, exultant singing) — the distinctive joy-shout of the sanctuary. Crucially, even the gôyim (nations) recognize what has happened: YHWH's action is visible enough that outsiders can see it and name its source.
3: higgîl yhwh laʿăśôt ʿimmānû hāyînû śĕmēḥîm — "the LORD has done great things for us — and we are overjoyed" — the psalm quotes the nations' speech (v2) and then adopts it as Israel's own confession. The verb higgîl (to do greatly, to act magnificently) in Hiphil — God's action is characterized by magnitude. śĕmēḥîm (joyful, glad) — the simple and direct emotional state that results from YHWH's great action. The brevity of v3 after the longer v2 is a poetic technique of emphasis: the main thing is stated cleanly.
4: šûbāh yhwh ʾet-šĕbîtênû kāʾăpîqîm bannegeb — "restore our fortunes, O LORD, like the streams that suddenly fill the desert wadis" — the prayer šûbāh ʾet-šĕbîtênû (restore our fortunes/captivity) mirrors v1's bĕšûb yhwh (when the LORD restored) — the past restoration becomes the grounds for praying it again. kāʾăpîqîm bannegeb (like the wadis in the Negev) — the ʾăpîqîm (channels, stream-beds, wadis) of the Negev are bone-dry for most of the year but can fill with thunderous torrents in minutes when rain falls in the hills. The petition is for that kind of sudden, dramatic reversal.
5: hazzōrĕʿîm bĕdimʿāh bĕrinnāh yiqṣōrû — "those who plant/sow in tears will harvest with shouts of joy" — the agricultural metaphor of sowing and reaping maps perfectly onto the exile/return pattern: the exile is the season of tears/planting; the return is the harvest of joy. zāraʿ (to sow, plant seed) — the farmer sowing in tears: perhaps the tears of depleted energy at day's end, or grief at the seeming impossibility of harvest, or the tears of repentance. bĕrinnāh yiqṣōrû (with shouts of joy they will reap) — future tense: this is still prophetic for some in the community.
6: hālôk yēlēk ûbākkōh nōśēʾ mešek-hazāraʿ bōʾ-yābōʾ bĕrinnāh nōśēʾ ʾălumōtāyw — "the one who sets out weeping, carrying the seed bag, will come home with shouts of joy, loaded with his sheaves" — the infinitive absolute constructions (hālôk yēlēk = goes and goes, i.e. really goes out; bōʾ-yābōʾ = comes and comes, i.e. really does return) emphasize the certainty of both the going and the return. mešek-hazāraʿ (the seed bag/precious seed, lit. "drawn-out seed" or "trail of seed") — the precious seed carried with tears. ʾălumōtāyw (his sheaves, bundles of grain) — the return is loaded, arms full. The theological promise: no sowing in tears is wasted; every act of faithful obedience in a season of loss will have its harvest.
